content: Antibiotic resistance occurs when bacteria evolve mechanisms to withstand antimicrobial drugs. The ESKAPE pathogens (Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterobacter species) represent the leading causes of multidrug-resistant infections globally. Resistance mechanisms include: enzymatic degradation (beta-lactamases), target modification (altered penicillin-binding proteins), efflux pumps that expel antibiotics, and reduced membrane permeability. Horizontal gene transfer via plasmids, transposons, and integrons accelerates spread between bacterial species.

Management of antibiotic-resistant infections requires: 1) Source control (drainage, debridement, device removal); 2) Empiric therapy based on local antibiogram and patient risk factors; 3) Definitive therapy guided by culture/sensitivity results; 4) Combination therapy for certain resistant organisms (e.g., carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae). Antimicrobial stewardship programs reduce resistance through: prospective audit with feedback, formulary restrictions, diagnostic stewardship, dose optimization using pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic principles, and de-escalation when cultures return. CDC Core Elements include leadership commitment, accountability, drug expertise, action, tracking, reporting, and education.

Diagnosis of antibiotic resistance involves: 1) Culture and susceptibility testing (disk diffusion, broth microdilution for MIC determination); 2) Rapid molecular tests detecting resistance genes (mecA for MRSA, vanA/vanB for VRE, KPC/NDM/OXA-48 for carbapenemases); 3) Phenotypic confirmation tests (combined disk tests for ESBL, carbapenemase production); 4) MALDI-TOF MS for rapid organism identification. Interpretive breakpoints follow CLSI or EUCAST standards. Screening cultures (nasal for MRSA, rectal for VRE/CRE) identify colonization in high-risk patients. Whole genome sequencing increasingly used for outbreak investigation and resistance mechanism elucidation.
